speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how you doing?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sh. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is tape number two with Mr. Isadore--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Horne, January 12, 1996, in San Diego. You were talking about right around the time when the war started against Russia, around June '41. That's where we just stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't take long-- three days. And the third day, I think, the Russian-- the German motorcycles were already in the city of Luck, just after three days. I tried to find out what happened with all those big shots over there from the restaurant, from the party people, what happened with them. And then I find out they all left. I went over there, and I looked at everything. It was just nothing. And they all left. The city was bombard every day. And after a few days, they stopped bombarding, because they were there already. They occupied the city. And no, don't ask how. We were scared to death. We knew there's nothing good will come out from that. But I-- my sister was still living over there in Luck. And she decided, because her husband, was when the Russians came in, they send him away. Because he was doing some business, which they didn't like it. He was buying and selling things like this. So they send him away somewhere to Russia. Which I never heard from him, and I never knew what happened with him, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know-- what was his name? Do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saul. His name was Saul, her husband. But we could never find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We could never found out. So my sister with the two children stayed with my mother in that village. When the Germans occupied, I was afraid. And they were talking. There was no ghetto yet made in Luck, and Luck was later a big ghetto. Because Luck get about probably 8,000, 10,000 Jewish people. It was a city of maybe about 25,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew they're going to make a ghetto. So I figured, I'm gonna probably too go home and stay home. Because it's more safer, I think, over there than over here in the big city.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, before I left, there was big announcements on the walls all over on the streets from the German government. All Jewish people in age from 18 to 40 should come and register in that and that place, in a certain space. And if not, then, of course, we know what's gonna happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So most of the people went and registered over there. I did not. For the reason I did not go and register-- they-- they-- I mean, in the announcement said just for work, to do some work, because it's a war. We got to help them out.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was at my plans to go home. So I figured, I'm not gonna register. I'm gonna go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what happened to all those people? They went to register in the thousands-- in the thousands. Nobody came back alive. They killed them right over there on the spot. How do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, somebody probably ran away, maybe. You know, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you heard about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Why did they do that? They did it, actually, more or less in every city. In every big city, they took out the young people-- the first-- in order, in case they should not resist later and fight against them. If they leave the old people that are women and children, they were more safer. Nothing will happen. That's the way they figured it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: The selections were backwards than in the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: So well, that's the way they-- they figured. And that's what happened that time in Luck. All the young people were killed before there was a ghetto. And later, they put it in the ghetto, the old people, the women, and the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get back to your hometown, then, without being caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I just went out. And I just walked a little bit. And then, if I see somebody with a horse and wagon, I ask them to put me-- give me a ride, something like that. And I came home. Ironically, that time already, Hitler start organizing the ghettos in 1941. From 1941 till 1942, that year was organized ghettos practically everywhere where were Jewish people lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: The idea from a ghetto-- I don't know if you know-- is the reason that, when the time comes, he had his plans to kill all those people. Then he wouldn't have no problem to go around and look if he's Jewish or if he's not Jewish, he's Polish or Ukrainian. This way, you know, he had them separated. They're all Jewish. Whenever he wants to kill them, he'll get them all out. All right? That's the way he planned it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was-- came home, it still didn't change too much, our life-- [LAUGHS] --in that small little village, like for the Russians, like for the Polish. We lived in our own house. There was no ghetto, because we-- they didn't have to make a ghetto in our place. And we lived whatever we had from the farm, like before. But suddenly, it's like the heaven fall down to the earth-- is when they came in one day. And we never see Germans-- only in Trochenbrod, in the next little town, was, I think, two or three officers staying there. Otherwise, is the Ukrainian police did all the job. They used to come with a bunch of the Ukrainian police, and that's all they did. They took out all the cows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cows?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cows-- all we had. They took them all, cleaned them out, and took them away. Because they had meat for free, you know. That was very bad for us, because that was our life, you know, to help us out. They have milk. They have cheese-- to sell the milk, you have a few bucks, you know, so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any option of resisting them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To resist?

speaker: interviewer
text: When they came by?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Nobody ever thought about resist. I don't know. Today, after, when I lived through the partisans and things like this, maybe I would be a-- I would be the first one to resist.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the problem, actually, to think about that-- if you would resist, let's say, and take those few guys, and kill them, and take away the-- the-- the rifles, right? And then-- and if anybody else comes, kill them too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that wouldn't be the solution. That would mean the end. They could have come with one tank and just destroyed the whole thing and killed us. I mean, you cannot fight with the-- with the government. On the other hand, our people didn't have any ammunition-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- that question that you're asking me now, I repeated thousands of times to the time since I survived. And where-- whenever we get together, I always says, my gosh. Why didn't we do that? Why didn't we do that? Why didn't we fought?

speaker: interviewee
text: One-- one rifle will bring another one. Another one will bring another one. Then we [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, then we came to the conclusion-- and what would be the end? We had no way to get food. So we would have to stay in the woods. And they would surrounded us. We would die from hunger-- either way, either way. That's-- so it was-- So what happened next after the Ukrainians came over?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, the next steps, they took away everything, I mean, from us. And-- and we still managed to live probably, I would say, maybe about another six months, you know? And just what we had in the fields. I mean, we starved a little. But-- but we didn't die from [INAUDIBLE]-- from hunger. I mean, we couldn't die, because we still had potatoes. You know, and if you have potatoes, and you have beans, and you have carrots, and you have all the other vegetables from the ground, you can survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After-- now also, at the same time, when we were living there quietly in that village, and also the other-- the next-- Trochenbrod, the next little town-- at the same time, they killed already tens of thousands of people in the big cities. And we didn't know about that. We didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody know what goes on in 10 mile-- kilometers away. Why? First of all, we were not allowed to walk nowhere or to drive anywhere, because we didn't have no transportation anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to wear either a Magen David or either a yellow sign, like a piece of rag, you know, pinned in to the front and to the back. For the purpose, if you walk around somewhere, and a German or a Ukrainian will see you, he can kill you. So he'll know that you are Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Otherwise, how would he know you were Jewish? The whole idea was just to make sure that easy to kill 'em. That's all the combinations what they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and one day, suddenly came in-- I don't remember if it was one or two Germans-- that's all-- to our place, to our village. But it was about-- about 20 or maybe 30 Ukrainian with the rifles-- everyone. These went to each house and said, there is a order from the German government to leave your place and go to the next place. Take whatever you can with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We don't care. So what are we going to take? Furniture-- old furniture--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --we gonna take? We cannot carry this anyway. So we took whatever we could, anybody had some bread in the house, whatever he has-- you know, carrots or things to eat-- and a pillow, you know, to put on the-- put some clothes, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I wanna tell you, we didn't have too many things anyway to take. Because we were very poor. We didn't have too much clothes. In our little town, if a person made himself a suit-- when he got threading, he wear them all his life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were very poor. We didn't have nothing to take anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the order came as they chased us out, all the hundred families from our village to the next one-- what I keep talking all the time about Trochenbrod, that little [NON-ENGLISH]. Now, to ghetto, our-- from our people and the people over there, we're already close to 4,000 people. Also, there were maybe [? about ?], I don't know, several hundred at least, refugees, which they ran away from the-- from the Warsaw area where the-- the Germans occupied in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came here when the Russians occupied to live here with the Russians. So they were here, too, in that little village. Because they figured it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a little small town, like I figured, everybody figured, oh, who's gonna bother us? Nobody's gonna bother us. They'll leave us alone. Maybe we will survive. You know, that's the way. So-- so we start walking. The whole-- all-- everybody, the whole little village left their houses, opened the doors. What do we need in the houses? We didn't know if our life was safe.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we start walking. Of course, there was no asphalt. That is sand, you know. And I still remember that episode, which I'll never forget, that Benjamin-- which I will tell you later, the story. He was hiding with me in a chimney. His father was walking. He was my neighbor and my cousin. And we lived across the street from the other one. He was always sticking next to me. He walked with me. And there in the middle of the road, he says to me, Itzhak, he says, I cannot walk anymore. He was, I don't know, maybe in the late 50s or 60 years old. I cannot walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said-- I said, what can I do? We didn't have no-- some wagon-- nothing. I say, sit down and rest a little bit. What can-- what can I tell him to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as he sat down, walked over the Ukrainian SS, [GERMAN]. You know, that was their expression, the Germans. [GERMAN] means, you ugly person. You're no good. That was their language, which they used to use to the Jewish people always. That's a German word, [GERMAN]. But the Ukrainian picked up that word from the Germans, you [GERMAN] Jew. And he just took out the gun. And he just killed him right on the spot. He said, you cannot go. He said, stay here-- right in the front of my eyes. Of course, we couldn't even do nothing. We couldn't even bury them. You couldn't do nothing, because if you would've stayed, he would have killed us, too. So we had to keep going. That's the way it was. We finally came to that place, Trochenbrod. And they-- they prepared for us, more or less, about, I don't know, maybe 40 homes for 4,000 people-- 40 homes. Push that in-- so many people in each house. And the-- we didn't have a place where to sleep for the-- we were sitting and sleeping. And whatever somebody had that we brought with us-- like I said, when we-- to eat, we ate. You ate. Whatever we didn't have, we didn't eat. A German officer used to take us out on the-- on the center of this little town. And he used to talk to us. Don't you worry, he said. We will take you. It's a war. We will take you men out. You have to work. You have to do some work, because it's a [? war ?]. The women and the children, we'll put it in in separate places. You know. And after the war, you'll be united-- just like that. Every morning, you used to come out. And I remember like today, even if it's more than 50 years. It was so hot-- miserable. In June, it's very hot that month, especially in Europe. It's very hot that month. And we used to sweat, and stay hungry, not eating. Were tired. And he used to say-- and that's the way the language he used to use for the big lie every morning, for three days. I can tell you the feeling that everybody got after going out and staying, and being hungry, and not knowing what's gonna be the next hour, we already gave up life. We said, the hell with it. It's not worth to fight it. Because that's the way you really get when you get so miserable. And you get so help-- helpless. And you cannot help yourself. And you-- and you feel like you gonna fall down and you gonna die anyway. That's the way you get the feeling in a moment like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that this is a lie that he was making up. Did you know that at the time? Or did you suspect that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or only later, did you realize?

speaker: interviewee
text: --we-- we know one thing-- that people are getting killed in many other cities. We didn't know exactly where and what it was. But rumors used to come around. The Germans are killing the Jewish people. This we know. We know that, if they came here and put us together, we know this isn't-- doesn't look good. There's something else to do with that-- with us. We know. We were scared to death. We knew. That's the way the feeling we had. What they did at the same time, those three days when they kept us, they took out all the workers which they-- the younger men and the people-- maybe about 100 of them. They took them out, and they separate them from the village. They couldn't get in into that village, this little ghetto which they made for the three days. They took them out further away somewhere. And they kept them like in a barrack surrounded with Ukrainian police. So they couldn't run away. They couldn't get away. And they couldn't get in touch with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were digging all day the grave for us. They couldn't even come and tell us. Well, of course, they wasn't sure either. They're digging the grave, and they ask, what is this for? And they told them that it's for military purposes. Everything was with lies. So in the-- in the period of three days, 100 people, they dig it out the grave. They're digging out the grave for us. And we didn't know that there was a grave preparing for us. And they didn't know that the grave is gonna be for us. And they kept them so isolated. They had no way how to get out. If you gonna ask me a question, why you were so many thousands of people in one place? Couldn't you, at night, just break through the gate or break through the-- to-- to-- to-- to the tents, whatever they had, and just run away to the woods? Yes, we could've done that. Maybe 1,000 would've been killed and maybe 2,000 not. But what would happen? Several thousand people, when they run away to the woods, what are you gonna do over there? Where are you gonna-- what are you gonna eat? It-- there was no way. There was no way. Again, and then beside that, the people were so weak from the three days. I knew I-- I-- I remember how I felt, which I was a young boy in the early 20s-- how weak, not eating, not drinking, not sleeping. How do you feel? You give up life.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't-- you don't have no more courage to fight. That's the truth. And people don't understand this. And it's very hard to understand unless you lived through this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after on the third day, suddenly, it was like 10:00 in the morning-- 10:00 in the morning. We hear noises from-- big trucks came in-- everything. And soon we look out through the window from the house. And we see that they take the women and the children and put them on the trucks. And then we also see that they-- 100 or 200 people at one time with a couple of Ukrainian police beside them, and they walk with them. Well, at this moment, of course, I didn't know where were they going and where the machines were going with the people. And we were, well, what-- what goes on here? I mean, we didn't-- we just really didn't know. But at that moment, when they were ready to come into our house to take all the people, I was there. And my-- and my sister starts screaming and crying. Why don't you go up here, the attic on the top? You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go over there. Maybe hide yourself. Maybe there is something where to hide. You know, you don't know what to do. So you look for something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Benjamin was there with me. We're standing together, because his family-- all his family-- sisters and brothers and so-- they all were in the same house with my family. My family-- my brother with his four children, my sister with her two children, and my mother-- that was my actually the real family. And then the other families from across the street, the neighbors there, they were all in the same house. So we jumped out. There was a ladder was standing over there. So we got up on the ladder, and we walked up on the top. We walked up. Over there was empty. Nothing was there. Where do you hide yourself? Because nothing is there. Usually, they used to keep straw and hay for food for the horses and cows. So in meantime, I see a-- the chimney. The chimneys in Europe was made-- was made of wood. A chimney was like a cupboard here, which you can walk in and stay inside. So I said to him, listen, it's a chimney. Let's go in in the chimney. So there was a little door made out from the board. So from time to time, they have to clean the chimney so the fire doesn't burn the house. So we got it inside, both of us. And we stand straight. We couldn't, of course, sit down. And we kept inside the little string to keep this little door, you know, closed. And it didn't take long, maybe about 10 minutes. I hear the crying and screaming when they start taking all the people from the house-- my-- my mother, my sister, and all the children. It was just unbearable. It was just unbelievable. We-- we said to each other, should we jump out and get out toward?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one thing I can tell you. If I would have been in the partisans before, we would have jumped out from the chimney and take that-- there was only one man there, a policemen. And we would kill him over there, just with our hand. We would grab him by his neck. And then we would've gotten the rifle with us. But I didn't know even, at that time, how to use a rifle. I didn't know. From my religious yeshiva, I didn't know-- I didn't learn how to use a rifle. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the way it happened. They took out all the people from there while we were standing over there and crying. And-- and we were staying there in the chimney until it got dark, from 10:00 in the morning. In summer, the days are long-- probably till 9 o'clock. It's about-- probably about 12 hours. And by that time, they managed to take out all the people except maybe some was hiding or ran away, you know, like we just happened-- a few, maybe. And they killed them all out. How did we know? There was one who ran away from there. And one lifted up the shovel and killed a German right on the spot. While he was standing and digging, you know, he was standing and watching. And he sees already what goes on.

speaker: interviewee
text: He figured, they're going to kill me right after that. So he picked up the shovel, hitted the German, and killed him. That was-- we found out later from one man that run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took them out-- the women, the kids, and all the men. They dressed them naked, because the idea also was, in meantime, they have the clothes. They can use it. Or somebody has a chain, or somebody has a watch. Somebody has something, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took out everything, what a person had, even the-- before they put into the grave, he says, the guy told me they opened their mouth and pulled out their golden teeth if anybody had it. What a vicious thing-- how people can be like that. And when the day was over, they finished the job-- 4,000 people buried in one-- in one grave. They covered it up with sand. And, of course, we didn't know those things while were staying in the chimney all day. We heard they were going back home. Everybody finished the job-- got drunk probably. And they were singing. They were happy-- singing, were happy, killed the people. When it got dark, me and Benjamin went out from the chimney. And we sit down. We didn't know where to go anyway, in the dark. So we sit down in the house till it got light.

speaker: interviewer
text: When it got light, we figure we have to go out and take a look and see what goes on here. Where are we? I mean, what are-- what are we gonna do here? So the first thing we decided, let's go. Then, we find some people over there. And they said, yeah, we know where all the people were killed. The people were killed not too far from here, maybe about one kilometer. We'll continue in-- in a moment.
